content.text: Customer Communication Templates for AI Outages

Anthropic's customer-facing communications during incidents follow a template system that standardises tone, structure, and disclosure scope while leaving room for the specifics of each event. Templates ensure that even under time pressure a Comms Lead never faces a blank page, and that each update meets a consistent quality bar for clarity, accuracy, and customer empathy.

This sub-subtopic covers the four canonical templates used for Claude API incidents, the field-by-field authoring rules for each, common mistakes to avoid, and the code patterns used to render and dispatch these messages through the status page and email pipeline.

attrs.alt: Decision tree for selecting the correct customer communication template: incident declared → external impact? → P0/P1? → privacy incident? → quality/safety degraded? — leading to TEMPL-1 through TEMPL-4

content.text: Four templates cover the majority of customer-facing scenarios. Each template maps to a set of trigger conditions. The Comms Lead selects the template, fills in the required fields, submits to the IC for approval, then publishes.
P0/P1, availability or latency degraded, no AI quality dimension
P0/P1, response quality, safety filters, or model behaviour degraded
P2/P3, subset of endpoints or regions affected, most customers unaffected
Any severity where customer data confidentiality or GDPR obligations triggered
Use TEMPL-1 when the Claude API is unavailable or severely degraded and there is no AI quality or privacy dimension. This is the most common P0/P1 template.

## [INVESTIGATING] Claude API Degraded — {start_time_utc}

We are currently investigating reports of elevated error rates and increased
latency affecting the Claude API.

**Impact**: Requests to {affected_endpoints} may return HTTP {error_codes} or
time out. API keys remain valid.

**Current status**: {one_sentence_current_finding}

**Next update**: We will post an update by {next_update_time_utc} or sooner
if the situation changes.

We apologise for the disruption to your work.
e.g. 503, 529 — list only codes seen in the last 5 minutes
Fact-only: 'We have identified elevated error rates in the us-east-1 region'

As the incident progresses, the INVESTIGATING header changes to IDENTIFIED or MONITORING. The Comms Lead edits the status page incident post in place — each update appends a timestamped entry rather than replacing the original.

--- Update 1 — {update_time_utc} ---
[IDENTIFIED] We have identified the root cause as {root_cause_summary}.
Engineering is deploying a fix. Impact: {current_impact_scope}.
Next update: {next_update_time_utc}.

--- Update 2 — {update_time_utc} ---
[MONITORING] The fix has been deployed. Error rates have returned to baseline.
We are monitoring for stability. Next update: {next_update_time_utc}.

--- Final — {resolved_time_utc} ---
[RESOLVED] The incident is resolved. Total duration: {duration}.
A post-mortem will be published within five business days.

TEMPL-2 applies when the Claude API is reachable but response quality is impaired: safety classifier false positives, unusual refusals, model behaviour regressions, or output quality drops below acceptable thresholds. This template requires special care because quality degradation is harder for customers to detect programmatically and more likely to cause silent failures in their pipelines.
Unexpected refusals on prompts that previously succeeded
Tool call JSON malformed or missing required fields
Multilingual prompts falling back to English
Safety filter false positives blocking benign content

Do not use vague symptom descriptions like 'unexpected outputs'. Name the specific failure mode the tech lead has confirmed. If the exact symptom is unknown, write: 'We are investigating reports of degraded output quality. The specific failure mode is under investigation.'

--- Final — {resolved_time_utc} ---
[RESOLVED] The quality degradation affecting {affected_capability} has been
resolved. All models are operating normally.

If you observed unexpected outputs between {start_time_utc} and
{resolved_time_utc}, we recommend reviewing your application logs for that
window. If you have questions about specific responses, please contact support
with your request IDs.

Total duration: {duration}. Post-mortem to follow within five business days.
TEMPL-3 covers P2 and P3 incidents where impact is bounded — a single region, a specific model version, or a subset of API endpoints. The tone acknowledges impact without conveying systemic failure.
The 'Unaffected' field is mandatory for TEMPL-3. Customers need to know whether they are impacted. 'The claude-3-haiku model is unaffected' is more useful than repeating what is affected.
Requests routed to eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1

TEMPL-4 applies whenever a customer data confidentiality concern exists: prompt data exposed to other tenants, logs retained beyond policy, conversation data accessible without authorisation, or any event that may trigger GDPR Article 33 or similar notification obligations. Legal review is mandatory before publishing TEMPL-4.
Article 33 requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a breach that poses a risk to individuals
Article 34 requires notification to affected data subjects without undue delay if the breach is likely to result in high risk
The 72-hour clock starts at 'becoming aware', not at confirmed scope — preliminary notification is required if the investigation is not complete
TEMPL-4 drafts must be reviewed by Legal before IC approval; the Legal sign-off step is mandatory and extends the normal IC-only approval gate
Post-incident: a full written report (DPA-compliant) is required within 5 business days for P0 privacy incidents
Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; creates legal exposure
'We have no confirmed reports of misuse at this time'
Minimising language undermines trust and may violate notification standards
Assigns blame to the customer before investigation is complete
Describe what happened technically without attributing cause
'We are investigating the full scope of the incident'

Customer communications are rendered via a Python pipeline that merges field values into templates, applies formatting, and dispatches through two channels: the status page API and the customer email system.

Template fields that contain times must be validated against a known format before dispatch. Free-text fields undergo a prohibited-phrase check to catch common authoring mistakes before the IC sees the draft.
Before submitting any customer update for IC approval, the Comms Lead runs through this checklist. Each item addresses a failure mode seen in past postmortems.
We will post an update by 14:45 UTC
We will post an update shortly
We have identified elevated error rates in eu-west-1
Something went wrong in our infrastructure
Retry with exponential backoff; requests to eu-west-1 can be rerouted to us-east-1
Affects /v1/messages requests using claude-3-opus; claude-3-haiku unaffected
We apologise for the disruption to your work
'There was no evidence of data misuse'
Email vs Status Page Channel Rules

Not every customer update warrants an email. Emails create higher urgency signals in customer systems and inboxes; overuse trains customers to ignore them. The channel selection rules below apply regardless of template.

Enterprise customers with SLA agreements receive email for P1 incidents in addition to status page posts. The customer tier list is maintained in the CRM and must be refreshed at the start of any P1 incident by the Comms Lead.

Within five business days of a P0 or P1 resolution, a public-facing incident report is published. This is not a postmortem document (which is internal) but a customer-appropriate summary covering what happened, timeline, customer impact, and actions taken to prevent recurrence.

The incident report goes through the same IC approval gate as real-time updates. A draft is circulated internally 48 hours before publication so the on-call engineer, Comms Lead, and IC can review accuracy. Legal reviews any incident report that involves a TEMPL-4 event.
Using 'we are aware' as the opening phrase — this is weaker than 'we are investigating' and conveys inaction
Omitting the next-update time commitment — customers will watch the status page and feel abandoned without a clear next timestamp
Stating 'some customers may be affected' for a P0 where impact is confirmed — hedge words undermine trust at the moment customers most need confidence
Conflating the status page post with the internal incident document — the status page post must never reference internal tool names, channel names, or on-call staff
Publishing a RESOLVED update before error rates have been stable for at least 15 minutes — premature resolution forces an embarrassing retraction
Copying a previous incident update verbatim without updating the timestamps — a detectable error that damages credibility

The four-template system (TEMPL-1 through TEMPL-4) covers general outages, AI quality degradation, partial outages, and privacy incidents. Each template has mandatory fields, prohibited phrases, and channel dispatch rules. TEMPL-4 requires Legal approval in addition to IC approval. The rendering pipeline validates all fields and enforces the prohibited-phrase list before submission. Postmortem communications follow the same approval gate as real-time updates and are published within five business days of P0/P1 resolution.
Select the template from the decision tree: external impact → severity → privacy → quality
Fill all required fields; never publish with unfilled placeholders
Run the lint checklist before submitting for IC approval
TEMPL-4 requires Legal sign-off; do not bypass for speed
Email only at declaration and resolution (P0/P1); intermediate updates are status-page-only
Postmortem report published within 5 business days of P0/P1 resolution
